title: Why Intuition Requires You to Be Honest with Yourself
meta_description: Discover why real intuition demands brutal self honesty. No fluff, just the truth about unlocking your inner knowing.
og_title: Why Intuition Requires You to Be Honest with Yourself
og_description: Discover why real intuition demands brutal self honesty. No fluff, just the truth about unlocking your inner knowing.
body:
The First Thing Nobody Tells You About Intuition

Let me start with something uncomfortable. You probably came here because you want to get better at hearing your intuition. You want clearer signals, stronger gut feelings, maybe even a direct line to something bigger than yourself. And that's fine. That's good. But here's the thing nobody tells you at the beginning: your intuition won't work for you if you're lying to yourself.

Not lying in the big, dramatic sense. Not the kind of lie where you're running a Ponzi scheme or telling your partner you're at the gym when you're really at a bar. I'm talking about the small, quiet lies. The ones you tell yourself every day without even noticing. The ones that feel like protection but are really just walls between you and your own knowing.

You know the ones I mean. "I'm fine" when you're not. "It doesn't matter" when it absolutely does. "I don't care" when you care so much it keeps you up at night. "They didn't mean it that way" when your gut is screaming that they absolutely did. Right?!

Here's what I've learned after years of working with people who are trying to wake up their psychic sensitivity: your intuition is not a magic trick. It's not a parlor game where you guess the card and win a prize. Your intuition is a relationship. And like any relationship, it only works when you're honest.

Your intuition doesn't speak to the version of you that pretends everything is fine. It speaks to the version of you that's willing to feel the full weight of what's actually true.

So let's get real about what this actually means. Because if you're anything like the people I work with, you've been told your whole life that you feel too much, that you're too sensitive, that you're making things up. And somewhere along the way, you learned to doubt yourself. To second-guess that first hit of knowing. To smooth things over so you don't rock the boat. And every time you did that, you taught your intuition that its voice doesn't matter.

Why Your Gut Keeps Quiet When You're Not Being Honest

Think about the last time you had a strong gut feeling about something. Maybe it was about a person. Maybe it was about a decision. Maybe it was about a situation that felt off even though you couldn't explain why. What did you do with that feeling?

If you're like most people, you probably talked yourself out of it. You told yourself you were being dramatic. You told yourself you were projecting. You told yourself that you don't have enough information to know. You told yourself that you're just anxious, that your nervous system is overreacting, that you need to calm down and think rationally.

And sure, sometimes that's true. Sometimes anxiety masquerades as intuition. But here's the thing: the only way to learn the difference between anxiety and intuition is to stop lying about what you're actually feeling. You can't sort through a mess you refuse to acknowledge exists.

Your intuition is like a muscle. If you only use it when you're sure it's right, it atrophies. If you ignore it when it whispers, it stops whispering. It learns that you're not a safe person to talk to. And honestly? It's right. If you're going to dismiss every uncomfortable truth your gut offers you, why would it keep offering them?

The Bright Wound: Where Your Honesty Lives

I call this work "The Bright Wound" for a reason. The bright wound is that part of you that got hurt because you could see what others couldn't. Because you could feel what others couldn't. Because you knew things before you had proof. That sensitivity got you into trouble. It made you different. It made people uncomfortable. And somewhere along the way, you learned to hide it.

But here's what nobody told you: that sensitivity is not your weakness. It's your antenna. It's the part of you that picks up on the things everyone else is pretending not to notice. And the only reason it hurts is because you're not supposed to have it in a world that rewards numbness.

So when I say intuition requires honesty, I mean it requires you to stop pretending that bright wound doesn't exist. It requires you to stop pretending that you don't feel things deeply. It requires you to stop pretending that you're fine when you're not. Because your intuition is not a separate thing from your sensitivity. They're the same thing. And if you're lying about one, you're blocking the other.

What Honesty Actually Looks Like in Practice

Okay, so let's get practical. What does it actually mean to be honest with yourself so your intuition can work? It's not about sitting in a dark room and having deep spiritual revelations. It's about the small, daily choices that build trust between you and your own knowing.

1. Stop Saying You're Fine When You're Not

This is the big one. The next time someone asks how you are, try saying something true instead of "fine." Try "I'm struggling today" or "I'm actually pretty anxious" or "I'm not sure how I am." Does that feel terrifying? Good. That's the point. Every time you say the truth out loud, you're telling your intuition that you're a safe person to talk to. You're proving that you can handle the truth. And your intuition notices that.

2. Name What You Actually Want

This one is sneaky. You probably think you know what you want. But most of us have been trained to want what we're supposed to want. A stable job. A good relationship. A nice house. But what do you actually want? What does your gut want? Not what sounds reasonable. Not what your mother would approve of. Not what your partner expects. What does the deepest part of you want?

If you can't answer that question honestly, your intuition is going to have a hard time guiding you. Because intuition doesn't just tell you what to avoid. It also tells you what to move toward. But it can't do that if you're pretending you don't want what you want.

3. Admit When You're Wrong

This one hurts. But it's essential. If you're going to build a relationship with your intuition, you have to be willing to admit when you got it wrong. When you ignored a gut feeling and it turned out to be right. When you followed a gut feeling and it turned out to be wrong. When you projected your fear onto a situation and called it intuition. When you used intuition as an excuse to avoid something hard.

The more honest you are about your mistakes, the more your intuition trusts you to handle the truth. And the more it trusts you, the clearer it gets.

The Connection Between Intuition and Self-Deception

Here's something I've noticed in my own work and in the people I teach: the people who struggle the most with intuition are often the people who are the most defended. They've built walls around their hearts. They've learned to rationalize everything. They've learned to explain away their feelings. They've learned to stay in their heads because their hearts have been broken too many times.

And I get it. I really do. Being sensitive in a world that doesn't value sensitivity is exhausting. It's easier to numb out. It's easier to pretend. It's easier to stay in your head where everything is logical and safe. But here's the thing: your intuition doesn't live in your head. It lives in your body. It lives in your gut. It lives in your heart. And if you've locked those doors, you've locked out your own guidance.

If you're looking for a practical way to start reconnecting with your body and your intuition, I'd recommend checking out Sonya Renee Taylor's "The Body Is Not an Apology" - it's a powerful book about radical self-love and reconnecting with your physical self, which is where your intuition actually lives.

What Happens When You Start Being Honest

I'm not going to lie to you: being honest with yourself is hard. It's uncomfortable. It means sitting with feelings you've been running from for years. It means admitting things about yourself that you're not proud of. It means letting go of the stories you've been telling yourself about who you are and why you do what you do.

But here's what happens on the other side: your intuition gets loud. Not in a dramatic, cinematic way. But in a quiet, steady, undeniable way. You start to know things before you have proof. You start to feel when something is off before you have evidence. You start to trust your first impression instead of second-guessing yourself into paralysis.

And the best part? You start to trust yourself. Not the version of yourself that's always trying to be right. Not the version that's always trying to be safe. But the real you. The one that knows things. The one that feels things. The one that's been there all along, waiting for you to stop pretending.

How to Start Practicing Honest Intuition Today

You don't need to meditate for an hour a day or go on a silent retreat to start this work. You can start right now, in this moment, with these three practices:

Practice 1: The Honest Check-In

Three times a day, stop and ask yourself: "What am I actually feeling right now?" Not what you think you should be feeling. Not what you wish you were feeling. What are you actually feeling? Name it. "I'm feeling anxious." "I'm feeling numb." "I'm feeling excited but scared." Whatever it is, just name it. No judgment. No fixing. Just honesty.

Practice 2: The Gut Check

Before you make any decision today, pause and ask your gut: "What do you want?" Not your brain. Your gut. And then listen. You might get a sensation in your stomach. You might get a feeling of expansion or contraction. You might get a word or an image. Whatever comes, just notice it. Don't act on it yet. Just practice receiving the information.

Practice 3: The Honest Confession

Find one thing you've been lying to yourself about. It can be small. "I'm actually exhausted." "I actually don't want to go to that party." "I actually am hurt by what they said." And say it out loud. To yourself. To a trusted friend. To a journal. Just say it. Feel the relief of not carrying that lie anymore.

For deeper work on understanding your emotional patterns and how they connect to your intuition, I highly recommend Dr. Judith Orloff's "The Empath's Survival Guide" - it's a practical manual for people who feel everything and need to learn how to work with it instead of against it.

The Hardest Truth You'll Face

Here's the hardest part of this whole thing: your intuition is not always going to tell you what you want to hear. Sometimes it's going to tell you that you need to leave a relationship you love. Sometimes it's going to tell you that you need to change careers when you're finally stable. Sometimes it's going to tell you that you need to set a boundary with someone you don't want to lose.

And that's where the honesty really matters. Because if you're not honest with yourself about what you're feeling, you'll rationalize your way out of following your guidance. You'll tell yourself that you're just scared. You'll tell yourself that you're being dramatic. You'll tell yourself that you don't have enough information. And you'll stay stuck in a life that doesn't fit you.

But if you're honest? If you're willing to feel the fear and do it anyway? If you're willing to admit that you know what you know even when it's inconvenient? That's when your intuition becomes your most reliable guide. That's when you start making decisions that actually align with who you are.

If you're looking for a complete guide to understanding and working with your intuitive gifts, Sonia Choquette's "Trust Your Vibes" is a classic for a reason - it walks you through the practical steps of developing your intuitive skills while staying grounded and honest.

The Myth of the Perfect Intuitive

One more thing before I wrap this up: there's no such thing as a perfect intuitive. You're going to get it wrong sometimes. You're going to confuse fear with intuition. You're going to project your hopes onto a situation and call it guidance. You're going to ignore a gut feeling and regret it. That's part of the process. That's how you learn.

The goal is not to be right all the time. The goal is to be honest all the time. Honest about what you're feeling. Honest about what you're afraid of. Honest about what you want. Honest about what you know and what you don't know. That's the foundation. That's what makes your intuition reliable. Not because it's always right, but because you're always willing to tell the truth about it.

For those of you who want to go deeper into the relationship between trauma, sensitivity, and intuitive development, Resmaa Menakem's "My Grandmother's Hands" is essential reading - it explores how trauma lives in the body and how healing that trauma opens up your natural intuitive abilities.

Where to Go From Here

If this article connected with you, if you felt that uncomfortable recognition in your chest, that's your intuition saying "pay attention." That's the part of you that knows it's time to stop pretending. That's the bright wound waking up.

The work is simple but not easy: be honest. Every day. In small ways and big ways. With yourself first, and then with others. Let your intuition know that you're a safe person to talk to. Let it know that you can handle the truth. And then watch what happens.

Your intuition has been waiting for you to get honest. It's been waiting for you to stop pretending. It's been waiting for you to trust yourself enough to hear what it's been trying to tell you all along.

The question is: are you ready to listen?

If you're not sure where to start, or if you want to go deeper into this work, I've created a free assessment that helps you identify where you're blocking your intuition and what specific step you need to take next. Take the Intuitive Honesty Assessment here - it'll give you a clear starting point based on where you actually are, not where you think you should be.
